title: Keynesian vs classical macroeconomics
summary:
## Two views of the macroeconomy

The central macro debate is how the economy behaves in the long run and whether it self-corrects — the **Classical (monetarist/new classical)** vs the **Keynesian** view. It shapes the whole argument over demand-side vs supply-side policy.

## The Classical view

- Markets (including labour) are **flexible** and **clear**; wages and prices adjust.
- **LRAS is vertical** at the full-employment (natural) level of output, set only by supply-side factors.
- Any fall in AD is **temporary**: falling wages/prices restore full employment automatically.
- A rise in AD in the long run just causes **inflation** (higher price level, same output) — so **demand management is ineffective** and may be inflationary.
- Policy focus: **supply-side** reform and controlling the money supply. Underpinned by the **Quantity Theory of Money (MV = PT)** — excessive money growth causes inflation.

## The Keynesian view

- Prices and especially **wages are ‘sticky’ downward**; markets may **not clear**.
- The economy can get **stuck in a prolonged recession** with a negative output gap (demand-deficient unemployment).
- The **Keynesian LRAS is reverse-L shaped**: horizontal with spare capacity, then upward-sloping, then vertical only at full capacity.
- Therefore **AD management works** when there is spare capacity: government spending can restore full employment.

## The multiplier and accelerator

- **The multiplier** — an initial injection (e.g. government spending) causes a **larger final rise in national income**, because spending becomes others' income and is re-spent. **Multiplier = 1 / (1 − MPC) = 1 / MPW**, where MPW = marginal propensities to save + tax + import. Bigger MPC → bigger multiplier. Works in reverse too.
- **The accelerator** — investment depends on the **rate of change** of output/demand; rising demand triggers a proportionately larger rise in investment (and vice versa), amplifying the cycle.

## The money supply and interest rates

- **Money supply** growth, the role of the central bank, and how monetary policy transmits to AD. Classical economists stress money growth → inflation; Keynesians note monetary policy can be weak in a **liquidity trap**.

## Synthesis and evaluation

Most economists accept a **middle ground**: demand-side policy helps in the short run/with spare capacity (Keynesian), but only **supply-side improvements raise output without inflation in the long run** (Classical). The right policy depends on the **cause of the problem and how much spare capacity exists** — the perfect evaluation framework.

## Why it matters

This debate ties AS macro (AD/AS, policy) into a coherent A2 framework and is the backbone of high-level policy essays: which LRAS shape you assume changes the whole conclusion.

keyTerms.definition: The view that markets clear and LRAS is vertical, so demand management is ineffective in the long run.
The view that sticky wages/prices can leave the economy below full employment, so AD management can work.
A reverse-L AS curve: horizontal with spare capacity, sloping up, then vertical at full capacity.
The ratio of the final change in national income to the initial change in spending; 1/(1−MPC).
The fraction of extra income that is spent; drives the size of the multiplier.
The theory that investment depends on the rate of change of output/demand.
The monetarist idea that excessive money-supply growth causes inflation.
When interest rates are so low that monetary policy loses traction on AD.

diagrams: **Classical vs Keynesian LRAS:** draw the vertical Classical LRAS and the reverse-L Keynesian LRAS; show that an AD rise causes only inflation on the vertical section but output gains on the horizontal section.
**The multiplier:** show an AD shift larger than the initial injection, illustrating the multiplied final effect on real output.
tips: Contrast the **two LRAS shapes** and explain how each changes the effect of a rise in AD — the crux of the debate.
Calculate the **multiplier = 1/(1−MPC) = 1/MPW** and explain why leakages (S, T, M) shrink it.
Link the **accelerator** to investment volatility and the trade cycle.
Reach a **synthesis judgement**: demand-side for spare capacity/short run, supply-side for long-run non-inflationary growth.
Note the **liquidity trap** and weak confidence as limits on monetary policy for evaluation.
